1:
The witnesses open the book by trying to fix its date, and their disagreement is instructive. John Gill surveys the field: Josephus places the history under Eli, but that is too late for Boaz, the grandfather of Jesse; Jarchi's identification of Boaz with the judge Ibzan is asserted “without proof”; the Jewish chronology, which carries the events as high as the times of Eglon king of Moab, when Ehud was judge, “comes nearer the truth,” and Dr. Lightfoot substantially agrees, slotting the story between the third and fourth chapters of Judges. Matthew Henry reports Bishop Patrick's preference for the days of Gideon, since only then do we read of a famine, by the Midianites' invasion (Judges 6:3–4), while Adam Clarke notes that Archbishop Ussher and most chronologers choose Shamgar. Yet Charles Ellicott, Henry, and John Wesley agree on the controlling datum: Boaz was born of Rahab (Matthew 1:5), so the events, as Ellicott puts it, “must be placed comparatively early in the period of the judges.”

On the famine itself the voices converge. Henry hears judgment in it: scarcity in that land flowing with milk and honey was one of the judgments God had threatened for sin (Leviticus 26:19–20) — “he has many arrows in his quiver” — and the sting is sharpest in Bethlehem, “which signifies the house of bread.” Clarke suggests a mundane instrument, the depredations of Philistines and Ammonites “carrying off the corn as soon as it was ripe,” and preserves the Targum's legend of ten great famines decreed upon the world before Messiah, this being the sixth. Ellicott sketches the geography of the escape: Moab, a land “of exceptional richness and fertility,” lay across the Dead Sea and the fords of Jordan; and Henry adds the bitter observation that common gifts of providence are often bestowed more plentifully on strangers to God — “Moab is at ease from his youth” (Jeremiah 48:11) — not because God loves Moabites better, but because they have their portion in this life.

The real division is moral: was Elimelech right to go? Henry weighs both sides and comes down against him. His care to provide for his family, taking wife and children with him, was “without doubt commendable” (1 Timothy 5:8); but the seed of Israel were now fixed and ought not to remove into heathen territory; he went out full (v.21), so the famine cannot have been desperate; and it is “an evidence of a discontented, distrustful, unstable spirit, to be weary of the place in which God hath set us,” for “it is seldom that changing our place is mending it.” Ellicott concurs more gently: how far the flight from the land of Jehovah to a land where Chemosh was worshipped could be justified “may well be doubted” — “it was disobeying the spirit of God's law” — even though God overruled it all for good. And there the witnesses find the deepest thing in the verse: while the judges ruled, some one city and some another, Providence, says Henry, “takes particular cognizance of Bethlehem, and has an eye to a King, to Messiah himself,” who, Wesley adds, would descend from two Gentile mothers, Rahab and Ruth.
2:
Here the storyteller pauses over names, and the commentators linger with him. John Gill reads Elimelech — “my God is King” — as a little confession of the constitution of the age: in the days of the judges the government was a theocracy, the judges raised up immediately by the Lord and ruling under him. He adds the Targum's note that Elimelech was “a great man,” and thinks it very likely, especially if, as the Jewish chronology has it, he was brother to Salmon, prince of the tribe of Judah — it is certain at least that Salmon's son Boaz was his kinsman. Keil and Delitzsch supply the philology: Elimelech (“to whom God is King”), Naomi (“the gracious”), Mahlon (“the weakly”), and Chilion (“pining”) are genuine Hebrew names, whereas Orpah and Ruth “cannot be satisfactorily explained from the Hebrew” — the popular gloss of Orpah as “turning the back” is “very arbitrary,” and the derivation of Ruth from a word for “friend” quite uncertain.

The sons' names carry the pathos. Adam Clarke renders them starkly — Mahlon, “infirmity”; Chilion, “finished, completed” — and Charles Ellicott thinks the names were given by reason of their feeble health and stand in the record “in reference to their premature death.” John Wesley draws the lesson that Matthew Henry shares almost verbatim: “probably they were sickly children, and not likely to be long-lived. Such are the products of our pleasant things, weak and infirm, fading and dying” — the pleasant one, Naomi, bears sickness and consumption. Ellicott also weighs the small critical question of seniority: Mahlon stands first here and in Ruth 1:5, Chilion first in Ruth 4:9; probably Mahlon, who was Ruth's husband (Ruth 4:10), was the elder.

“Ephrathites of Bethlehem-judah” roots the family in sacred geography. Keil and Delitzsch derive the gentilic not from Ephraim, as in Judges 12:5, but from Ephrath, “the old name for Bethlehem” (Genesis 35:19; so of Jesse in 1 Samuel 17:12), so that Ephrathite means simply sprung from Bethlehem; Wesley agrees that “Bethlehem was otherwise called Ephratha,” and Ellicott notes that “Judah” is added to distinguish the town from the Bethlehem of Zebulun (Joshua 19:15) — though why the narrator pairs the newer town-name with the older gentilic, he confesses, “does not appear.” The combined point of the witnesses is quietly theological: a family whose father's name declares God King, sprung from the house of bread in the town of David's line, goes down to Moab and “continued there” — and every name in the verse will be weighed by the story that follows, until the pleasant one asks to be called bitter and the unexplainable Moabite name becomes the title of a book in Israel's Scriptures.
3:
The first death falls with narrative suddenness, and the commentators' first question is when. Adam Clarke is briefest: Elimelech died “probably a short time after his arrival in Moab.” John Gill sets out the alternatives: Josephus says he died after dwelling in the land ten years and after marrying his two sons to Moabitish women, but Gill sides with the Jewish expositor Alshech, observing that the text places the marriages after the father's death — while he lived, the sons were not married to them — and that the “about ten years” of v.4 measures the sons' dwelling in the land, “so that it is most probable that their father died quickly after he came into the land of Moab.” Keil and Delitzsch let the clause speak for itself — “thus the woman remained left alone of her two sons and her husband” is the pattern toward which the paragraph moves — this verse being the first tightening of the knot that closes at v.5.

Matthew Henry supplies the reflection the terse narrative invites. Wherever we go, “we cannot out-run death, whose fatal arrows fly in all places”; and “we cannot expect to prosper when we go out of the way of our duty. He that will save his life by any indirect course shall lose it.” For Henry the death of Elimelech in the land he fled to is the beginning of the wasting of the family that would not take its lot with its brethren; and Gill's plain summary of the survivors — “in a strange land, she without a husband, and they without a father” — is a picture of the insecurity of every earthly refuge. The man who left the house of bread to escape dying found death waiting in the fields of plenty.

Yet the witnesses do not let judgment stand alone. Henry watches the widow's heart: after her husband's death Naomi “took so much the more complacency and put so much the more confidence in her sons,” living under the shadow of these surviving comforts — gourds, he calls them, green and growing up in the morning, that would presently wither. Death, when it comes into a family, “often makes breach upon breach”; one is taken away, and if that affliction is not duly improved, another of the same kind follows. The verse thus stands as the hinge between emigration and desolation: the severe mercies of the God who, before the book ends, will be blessed for not leaving Naomi without a kinsman (Ruth 4:14) begin here, in a grave in Moab.
4:
The marriages to Orpah and Ruth divide the witnesses sharply, and the Targum's verdict is quoted on every side: “They transgressed the decree of the word of the Lord, and took to themselves strange wives.” Adam Clarke calls the paraphrase “very proper”; Charles Ellicott makes it a family declension — “the fault of settling on a heathen soil begun by the father is carried on by the sons in marrying heathen women,” incurring the further risk of idolatry, “as King Solomon found”; and Matthew Henry pronounces that “all agree that this was ill done,” warning that those who bring young people into bad acquaintance, and take them out of the way of public ordinances, “know not what they do, nor what will be the end thereof.”

Against the condemnation stands a real legal and historical question. Keil and Delitzsch point out that marriage with the daughters of Moab “was not forbidden in the law” as marriages with Canaanitish women were; and Aben Ezra, reported by John Gill, thought the women were proselyted to the Jewish religion before the marriages. Gill himself splits the difference — this “seems plainly to be the case of Ruth; at least she was so afterwards, if not before” — while Henry counters from the story itself: Orpah is said to return “to her gods” (v.15); “the gods of Moab were hers still.” John Wesley leaves the alternatives standing side by side: “either these were proselytes when they married them, or they sinned in marrying them, and therefore were punished with short life, and want of issue.”

On Ruth herself the notes gather. Ellicott derives her name as either “comeliness” or “companion,” the Syriac spelling favouring the latter, and identifies her as the wife of Mahlon (Ruth 4:10), apparently the elder son. The Targum's claim that Ruth was the daughter of Eglon king of Moab he sets aside as “obviously from the wish to exalt the dignity of Ruth,” and Henry calls it a groundless tradition that cannot even cohere with the rabbis' other tradition that Boaz was Ibzan, who judged Israel long after Eglon's death (Judges 12:8). And Gill states the resolution in which all the witnesses can rest: “however, it was so permitted by the Lord, and ordered in Providence, that from one of them the Messiah might spring.” The perilous match — unlawful in the Targum's eyes, unwise in everyone's — becomes, in the overruling of God, a thread in the genealogy of Matthew 1:5: grace writing straight on the crooked lines of an exiled family's choices.
5:
Now the blow falls double: “and Mahlon and Chilion died also both of them,” after about ten years in the land, and the Targum again presses its moral — “because they transgressed the decree of the Word of the Lord, and joined in affinity with strange people, their days were cut off.” Precisely here the witnesses divide over how much weight that reading can bear. Adam Clarke endorses it almost outright: “it is very likely that there is more here than conjecture.” Charles Ellicott counsels reserve: the men died “clearly as quite young men,” but “it is not for us to say how far those are right who see in the death of Elimelech and his sons God's punishment for the disregard of His law.” John Gill reports the Targum's judgment without quite adopting it, and John Wesley had already offered the punishment reading as one horn of his alternative on v.4: if they sinned in marrying, they “were punished with short life, and want of issue.”

What no witness disputes is the desolation. Wesley gathers the verse's whole weight into Naomi's person: “loss of children and widowhood are both come upon her. By whom shall she be comforted? It is God alone that is able to comfort those who are thus cast down.” Matthew Henry, tracing the same lines, hears the prophet's language of the doubly bereaved — these two things coming upon her in a moment, in their perfection (Isaiah 47:9) — and observes again that death in a family “often makes breach upon breach”: one affliction not duly improved, and God sends another of the same kind. The sons were buried “soon after they were married, for neither of them left any children. So uncertain and transient are all our enjoyments here.”

The pastoral point is where the witnesses land together. Henry: it is therefore our wisdom “to make sure of those comforts that will be made sure and of which death cannot rob us.” The narrative has now stripped Naomi to nothing — husband, sons, posterity, homeland all gone — precisely so that what follows may be read as pure gift. Whether or not the early graves were judgment (Clarke inclining to say yes, Ellicott refusing to say), the woman “left of her two sons and her husband” stands at the story's lowest point, and every grace to come — Ruth's clinging, Boaz's kindness, Obed on her bosom — will be measured against the emptiness of this verse.
6:
The turn of the book begins with a rumour of bread. Keil and Delitzsch read the motive clause exactly: Naomi rose up out of the fields of Moab because she had heard “that Jehovah had visited His people, i.e., had turned His favour towards them again to give them bread.” Matthew Henry makes the visitation a doctrine: though God contend long, he will not contend always; “plenty is God's gift, and it is his visitation which by bread, the staff of life, holds our souls in life.” John Wesley compresses Naomi's obedience into a clause — bread means simply food, “so she staid no longer there than necessity forced her” — and Adam Clarke preserves the Targum's embroidery: she heard “by the mouth of an angel,” and the Lord visited his people “because of the righteousness of Ibzan the judge, and because of the supplications of pious Boaz.” Clarke also passes on the curious conjecture that Mahlon and Chilion are the Joash and Saraph who “married in Moab” in 1 Chronicles 4:22.

Why now, and not before? Henry probes her heart with two answers. She took the deaths of her sons as “a judgment upon her family for lingering in the country of Moab,” and “hearing this to be the voice of the rod, and of him that appointed it, she obeys and returns,” like the chastened wife who says, I will go and return to my first husband (Hosea 2:7); indeed, “had she returned upon the death of her husband, perhaps she might have saved the life of her sons.” And Moab had become “a melancholy place to her”: it is with little pleasure that she can go on breathing air in which her husband and sons expired. Then comes the sentence this verse exists to teach: “Thus God takes away from us the comforts we stay ourselves too much upon… Earth is embittered to us, that heaven may be endeared.” Charles Ellicott adds the tender conjecture that “her conscience smote her for distrusting the mercies of the God of Israel,” and John Gill that she longed for her own country, “where she would have better opportunities of serving the Lord,” having no heart to stay in an idolatrous land.

Henry presses the general rule Naomi embodies: “though there be a reason for our being in bad places, yet, when the reason ceases, we must by no means continue in them.” Forced absence from God's ordinances is an affliction; the same absence continued by choice “becomes a great sin.” On the daughters-in-law who arose with her, Gill enters the caution that becomes the next verses' debate: at their first setting out it does not appear they intended more than to accompany her part of the way — while Keil and Delitzsch notice that the narrator's word “return,” strictly taken, “only applies to Naomi,” the Moabite women never having seen Judah at all. The verse holds the book's theology in miniature: the Lord who breaks is the Lord who visits, and the same hand that emptied Naomi has already begun to fill her.
7:
The narrator walks slowly here — she went forth out of the place where she was, her two daughters-in-law with her, and they went on the way — and the commentators notice both the geography and the grammar. John Gill ponders why the family's home is never named but is only “the country,” literally the field, of Moab: perhaps the Moabites would not suffer Israelites to dwell in their cities, “only allowed them to pitch their tents in their fields”; or perhaps they chose the fields themselves, “that they might as much as possible avoid conversation with them, and be preserved from their idolatry, and other corruptions.” Keil and Delitzsch mark the word that closes the verse: “return,” taken strictly, “only applies to Naomi, who really returned to Judah, whilst her daughters-in-law simply wished to accompany her thither.”

That observation opens the verse's one real debate: what did Orpah and Ruth intend when they set out? Gill thinks they meant at first only to go some part of the way, “for it does not appear to be their intention, at least at first setting out, to go with her into the land of Canaan.” Charles Ellicott reads the opposite: “both clearly purposing to go with Naomi to the land of Israel (Ruth 1:10), not merely to escort her a little way,” since such a parting “could hardly but be final.” Either way, the witnesses agree on what the shared road proves. Naomi “had obviously won the affections of her daughters-in-law” (Ellicott); and Matthew Henry reads the escort as testimony to years of quiet kindness — they would bring her on her way and help carry her luggage, “for it does not appear that she had any servant to attend her,” and “it was a sign they had dwelt together in unity, though those were dead by whom the relation between them came.”

Henry widens the scene into household counsel. Naomi, “as became an Israelite, had been very kind and obliging to them and had won their love, in which she is an example to all mothers-in-law”; and though the young widows still retained an affection for the gods of Moab (v.15) while Naomi was faithful to the God of Israel, difference of religion “was no hindrance to either side from love and kindness, and all the good offices that the relation required.” Mothers-in-law and daughters-in-law are too often at variance (Matthew 10:35), “and therefore it is the more commendable if they live in love.” So three widows walk one road with very different distances in their hearts — and the road itself, Ellicott will note at v.19, ran some fifty hard miles across the Arnon and the Jordan.
8:
At what John Gill supposes to be “the utmost limits of the land of Moab,” on the borders of Israel, Naomi turns and speaks the first of her dissuasions — and the commentators are unanimous about its spirit. Charles Ellicott names the quality exactly: “Naomi's love is all unselfish. The company of Ruth and Orpah would clearly have been a great solace to her, yet she will not sacrifice them to herself.” They each had a mother and a home; a home was the one thing Naomi could not promise them.

Why “her mother's house,” when Ruth's father at any rate was still living (Ruth 2:11)? Keil and Delitzsch answer in a phrase that has become the standard comment: “because maternal love knows best how to comfort a daughter in her affliction.” John Wesley adds the domestic realism of the ancient East: daughters conversed most frequently with their mothers and dwelt “in the same apartments with them,” distinct from the men's quarters. The phrase is no slight upon the fathers but a widow's precision about where wounded women are healed.

The blessing itself is the heart of the verse: “the LORD deal kindly with you, as ye have dealt with the dead, and with me.” Matthew Henry unfolds the dismissal as a model parting. First, commendation — “you were good wives to your husbands that are gone, and have been good daughters to me” — for praise is “a debt owing to those who have conducted themselves well in any relation,” and it is very comfortable, when relations part, to have both their testimony and the testimony of our own consciences that we did our duty while we were together. Then, prayer — “it is very proper for friends, when they part, to part with prayer.” Henry marks that Naomi names Jehovah, Israel's God and the only true God, “that she might direct her daughters to look up to him as the only fountain of all good,” and that her petition rests on a settled maxim of providence: “it may be expected and prayed for in faith that God will deal kindly with those that have dealt kindly with their relations. He that watereth shall be watered also himself.” Wesley notes soberly that “the dead” are her sons, their husbands, “while they lived.” The witnesses converge on the wonder of the scene: a bereaved Israelite widow with nothing left to give — silver and gold she had none, Henry observes, only a parting kiss — gives the one gift that outlasts estates: she puts her Moabite daughters-in-law under the kindness of the covenant God, and the rest of the book, in Ruth's case, is the answering of that prayer beyond all her asking.
9:
The general blessing now sharpens to a particular one: “the LORD grant you that ye may find rest, each of you in the house of her husband.” Charles Ellicott hears deliberate structure: a twofold blessing, “made the more solemn by the twofold mention of the sacred name Jehovah” — mercy in general at v.8, and now “the special blessing, that they may find rest and peace in a new home.” John Gill reviews the ancient attempts to supply an object for “grant you” — the Targum's “a perfect reward,” Aben Ezra's “an husband,” Josephus's paraphrase that she wished them happier marriages than they had with her short-lived sons — and rules them all needless: “what follows is connected with the wish, and contains the sum of it.” Keil and Delitzsch render that sum plainly: “that ye may both be happily married again.”

Matthew Henry lingers over the word rest. The married state is “a state of rest, such rest as this world affords” — rest in the house of a husband beyond what a mother's or a mother-in-law's house can give; and it is fit, by the apostle's own direction concerning young widows, that they “marry, bear children, and guide the house” (1 Timothy 5:14). It is a pity, he adds, that those who have approved themselves good wives should not again be blessed with good husbands. But he refuses to let the blessing terminate on earth: “this rest is God's gift… If any content and satisfaction be found in our outward condition, God must be acknowledged in it… Yet let God be the rest of the soul, and no perfect rest thought of on this side heaven.” That a destitute mother-in-law should pray her Moabite daughters into new homes — should wish them, in effect, out of her own family and into others' — is for Henry and Ellicott alike the measure of her unselfishness.

“Then she kissed them; and they lifted up their voice, and wept.” Keil and Delitzsch note the kiss as the customary token of leave-taking (Genesis 31:28); Henry sees more: the parting kiss is “the seal of such a true friendship” as Naomi will retain the pleasing remembrance of while she lives — “if relations must part, let them thus part in love, that they may (if they never meet again in this world) meet in the world of everlasting love.” And the story keeps its own quiet account of the prayer: the God whom Naomi invoked grants Ruth rest indeed, but in Bethlehem rather than Moab — Naomi herself taking up the same word when she asks, “shall I not seek rest for thee, that it may be well with thee?” (Ruth 3:1).
10:
The daughters-in-law answer with one voice: “surely we will return with thee unto thy people.” John Gill notes the small human detail the narrative implies — they said it “when they had eased themselves in cries and tears, and had recovered their speech.” The protest rises out of the weeping of v.9: grief first finds vent, and only then does resolution find language. Keil and Delitzsch hear the same intensity in the Hebrew: the particle with which their reply opens “serves to strengthen” the declaration — the emphatic surely of the English — so that the sentence stands almost as a vow: not “perhaps we shall come,” but “we will return with thee.”

Matthew Henry gives the verse its fullest weighing. That both women say it is “a rare instance of affection to a mother-in-law and an evidence that they had, for her sake, conceived a good opinion of the people of Israel.” The persuasive power at work in the verse is nothing other than Naomi's character: her good conversation had “won upon them,” so that the God and people these Moabite women knew only through one bereaved and impoverished widow seemed worth casting in their lot with. But Henry will not let the identical words conceal the unequal hearts that speak them: “even Orpah, who afterwards went back to her gods, now seemed resolved to go forward with Naomi. The sad ceremony of parting, and the tears shed on that occasion, drew from her this protestation, but it did not hold. Strong passions, without a settled judgment, commonly produce weak resolutions.”

The verse is thus the story's moment of indistinguishability. To any onlooker on the Moab road, Orpah and Ruth stand identically committed; both weep, both refuse to turn, both say “thy people” — though neither has yet said “thy God.” What separates them will appear only under Naomi's deliberate testing in vv.11–13, and the same tears will fall again at v.14, where the kiss finally parts from the cleaving. Meanwhile the witnesses teach the church to prize, and to test, warm beginnings: affection for the people of God, kindled by the credit one believer's life gives to the whole better country, is a fair morning — but only a settled judgment, Henry insists, makes it day; and the profession that is drawn out by a scene of tears must yet prove itself when the scene is over.
11:
Naomi's first dissuasive rests on an institution her daughters evidently knew: “are there yet any more sons in my womb, that they may be your husbands?” Adam Clarke explains that this was spoken “in allusion to the custom, that when a married brother died without leaving posterity, his brother should take his widow,” the children of such a marriage being accounted the dead brother's. John Wesley traces the custom to the ancient practice (Genesis 38:8) and “the express law of God” (Deuteronomy 25:5), “which doubtless she had acquainted them with before, among other branches of the Jewish religion.” Keil and Delitzsch unpack the rhetorical question with legal precision: “I am not pregnant with sons, upon whom, as the younger brothers of Mahlon and Chilion, there would rest the obligation of marrying you,” according to the levirate law.

But why dissuade at all? John Gill supplies the motive the narrative leaves implicit: Naomi supposed their resolution “only arose from a natural affection, and not from any love to the God or people of Israel; at least doubting whether it was so or not,” and she was willing to try whether either or both were “really from a principle of religion inclined to go with her,” lest they should afterwards repent, apostatize, “and bring a reproach upon the true religion.” Clarke, for his part, admires the pleading itself — “there is something very persuasive and affecting in the address of Naomi to her daughters-in-law” — and itemizes its logic: she has no other sons to give them; she is not with child; she is too old for a husband; and even should sons be born that night, the widows could not wait for them.

Charles Ellicott hears beneath the argument the pathos of the arguer. Since her advice thus far “is insufficient to shake the affectionate resolve of the two women, she then paints the loneliness of her lot.” They, in spite of their great loss, are young, and from their mothers' houses “may again go forth to homes of their own. She, old, childless, and solitary, must wend her weary way back to live unaided as best she may.” The dissuasion, in other words, costs Naomi everything it wins: every argument she makes against their coming is a fresh confession of her own desolation. The witnesses accordingly read the verse two ways at once, and both are true — as the unselfishness of a woman who would rather be alone than be a burden, and, with Gill, as the sifting providence of God, who through Naomi's very discouragements is distinguishing the proselyte of love from the companion of tears.
12:
Naomi repeats the charge — “turn again, my daughters, go your way” — and then argues it to its limit: “for I am too old to have an husband. If I should say, I have hope, if I should have an husband also to night, and should also bear sons…” John Gill reads the repetition itself as deliberate: “this she repeated still to try their affections to her, and especially whether there was any real love to the God of Israel, his people, and worship” — the same sifting he found in v.11, now pressed a second time upon hearts that had withstood it once. Keil and Delitzsch paraphrase the escalation: not only is there no levirate hope in sons already conceived; there is no prospect at all — “I have no prospect of having a husband and being blessed with children.” The hypothetical is deliberately extravagant: a husband this very night, conception, sons — and even granting the impossible, the widows' waiting would be beyond all reason.

Matthew Henry catches the tone of the self-portrait: it became her age “to think of dying and going out of the world, not of marrying and beginning the world again.” And beyond the levirate she could not even maintain them: the greatest grievance of her poverty, Henry observes, was “that she was not in a capacity to do for them as she would” — she could more easily want herself than see her daughters want. The old woman's arithmetic of impossibilities is thus love's arithmetic: she counts up her own hopelessness so that they will not mortgage their youth to it.

Henry also raises, and answers, the pastoral scruple the whole dissuasion provokes: did Naomi do well to discourage two souls she might have brought to the faith and worship of the God of Israel? She desired their conversion, no doubt; “but, if they did come with her, she would not have them to come upon her account. Those that take upon them a profession of religion only in complaisance to their relations, to oblige their friends, or for the sake of company, will be converts of small value and of short continuance.” She would have them “sit down first and count the cost,” as our Saviour dealt with the forward scribe — Master, I will follow thee whithersoever thou goest — who was answered with the foxes' holes and the Son of man's homelessness (Matthew 8:19–20). “It is good for us to be told the worst”; thoughts ripened into resolves by serious consideration are likely to keep, “whereas what is soon ripe is soon rotten.” The verse's severity is the severity of honest evangelism: Naomi will own no disciple whom the truth has not first tried.
13:
Two questions and a cry. On the questions John Gill anchors the realism: would they tarry for unborn sons “till they were grown”? It is not to be thought — though Tamar indeed tarried for Shelah (Genesis 38:11), “yet he was born, and of some years of age, though not a grown man”; Naomi's case admits no such waiting, for the sons in question do not exist.

The cry — “it grieveth me much for your sakes that the hand of the LORD is gone out against me” — divides the witnesses over translation before it unites them in doctrine. Charles Ellicott argues that “a much more probable translation is, it is far more bitter for me than for you,” finding an exact grammatical parallel in Genesis 19:9 and the support of the Septuagint, the Peshito Syriac, and the Targum against the looser rendering of the Vulgate that lies behind the English. On that reading Naomi compares griefs: the young may be married again; she is bereaved beyond repair. John Wesley and Matthew Henry expound the received text and draw from it one of the chapter's finest spiritual lessons. Wesley: the grief is for them — “that you are left without the comfort of husbands or children; that I must part with such affectionate daughters; and that my circumstances are such, that I cannot invite you to go along with me” — for so mean was her condition that Ruth, arrived at Bethlehem, “was forced to glean for a living.” Henry: she was the sinner, but they were the sufferers; “a gracious generous spirit can better bear its own burden than it can bear to see it a grievance to others.”

Both readings meet in the clause no one disputes: “the hand of the LORD is gone out against me.” Wesley: “It is with me, that God has a controversy. This language becomes us, when we are under affliction; tho' many others share in the trouble, yet we are to hear the voice of the rod, as if it spake only to us.” Henry says the same at length: she judges herself “chiefly aimed at in the affliction,” not billeting the rebukes of the rod at other people's houses but taking them to herself. And Wesley closes with the evangelistic frame he shares with Henry: did she not wish to bring them to the worship of the God of Israel? “Undoubtedly she did. But she would have them first consider upon what terms, lest having set their hand to the plow, they should look back” (Luke 9:62). Naomi under the rod neither curses God nor flatters her would-be converts — and the witnesses commend both severities as grace.
14:
The parting of the ways is told in two verbs. “And Orpah kissed her mother in law; but Ruth clave unto her.” John Gill hears the renewed weeping first — they lifted up their voice and wept again, “not being able to bear the thought of parting.” Adam Clarke notes that the Septuagint completes the first clause — “and returned to her own people” — with the Vulgate, Syriac, and Arabic to the same purpose. Charles Ellicott is careful to be fair to Orpah: though “unwilling to leave her mother-in-law” and “warmly attached to her,” she “still thinks of the hardships of the journey, of the hardships when the journey is done; and the comforts of home detain her.” Keil and Delitzsch weigh the second verb: Ruth “clung to her” — the word of Genesis 2:24, where a man leaves father and mother to cleave to his wife — “forsaking her father and mother to go with Naomi into the land of Judah” (Ruth 2:11).

The kiss is where the witnesses preach. John Wesley: Orpah “departed from her with a kiss. Bade her farewell for ever. She loved Naomi, but she did not love her so well, as to quit her country for her sake. Thus many have a value for Christ, and yet come short of salvation by him, because they cannot find in their hearts, to forsake other things for him. They love him, and yet leave him, because they do not love him enough, but love other things better.” Matthew Henry, from whom Wesley's words descend almost verbatim, adds the gospel type — the young man who went away from Christ sorrowful (Matthew 19:22) — and the double effect of a single sermon: Naomi's honest dissuasion was to Orpah “a savour of death unto death,” furnishing her an excuse to go home, and to Ruth “a savour of life unto life,” strengthening her resolution (2 Corinthians 2:16).

So the same tears, the same road, the same mother-in-law — and opposite directions for eternity. The witnesses do not vilify Orpah: Ellicott's portrait is sympathetic, and Henry grants that her kiss showed real affection and that she was loth to part. That is precisely their warning — the almost-disciple is not a monster but an ordinary, loving person whose love stops at the border of sacrifice. Ruth's cleaving, by contrast, is already covenantal before she has spoken a word: the narrator has chosen the marriage-verb, as Keil and Delitzsch imply, because what Ruth does to Naomi is what conversion does to God — a leaving, a cleaving, and no way back.
15:
Naomi turns Orpah's departure into a final test: “behold, thy sister in law is gone back unto her people, and unto her gods: return thou after thy sister in law.” The clause “unto her gods” raises two questions the witnesses debate. First, what was Orpah? Adam Clarke is blunt: “they were probably both idolaters, their having been proselytes is an unfounded conjecture. Chemosh was the grand idol of the Moabites. The conversion of Ruth probably commenced at this time.” Matthew Henry reads Orpah's future the same way: whatever respect she showed Israel's God while she lived with Naomi, “it would be next to impossible for her to show any respect to the God of Israel when she went to live among the worshippers of Chemosh” — and he coins the abiding warning: “Those that forsake the communion of saints, and return to the people of Moab, will certainly break off their communion with God, and embrace the idols of Moab.”

Second — and here is the sharper division — what did Naomi mean by naming Moab's gods without protest? Charles Ellicott takes the words at their hardest: “Naomi doubtless views the Moabite idols as realities, whose power is, however, confined to the land of Moab. She is not sufficiently enlightened in her religion to see in the Lord more than the God of Israel” — a bold reading that makes Naomi a woman of her age, her faith real but her theology still bounded by borders. John Wesley will have none of it: “this she saith, to try Ruth's sincerity and constancy, and that she might intimate to her, that if she went with her, she must embrace the true religion.” John Gill stands with Wesley: the whole speech proceeds from Naomi's desire to prove whether love to the God of Israel, and not mere affection to herself, carried Ruth forward.

Henry supplies the words he imagines beneath Naomi's words: “If ever thou wilt return, return now. This is the greatest trial of thy constancy; stand this trial, and thou art mine for ever.” And he generalizes Orpah into a category the church knows well: “such offences as that of Orpah's revolt must needs come, that those who are perfect and sincere may be made manifest, as Ruth was upon this occasion” — the apostle's principle in 1 Corinthians 11:19 working itself out on the Moab road. Whether Naomi's theology was clouded, as Ellicott supposes, or her strategy deliberate, as Wesley, Gill, and Henry insist, the effect is the same and all confess it: the last dissuasion strips the choice to its religious core — people and gods together — so that when Ruth answers in the next verse, she answers not about geography but about God.
16:
On Ruth's confession the witnesses vie with one another in admiration. Adam Clarke: “a more perfect surrender was never made of friendly feelings to a friend” — and he walks the clauses: I will follow thee; I will take the same fare with which thou meetest; “I most cheerfully abandon my own country”; “I will also henceforth have no god but thy God, and be joined with thee in worship, as I am in affection and consanguinity.” Matthew Henry: “nothing could be said more fine, more brave, than this,” and he insists on its source — “it is an instance of the grace of God inclining the soul to the resolute choice of the better part.” Her mother-in-law's dissuasions, like Joshua's “you cannot serve the LORD” (Joshua 24:19), only made her the more resolute. Charles Ellicott: “Ruth's nobleness is proof against all,” and the intensity of her feeling “comes out all the more strongly now that she pleads alone” — the toilsome journey, the hard lodging, the strange people, the new God, all accepted in a breath.

The philology serves the devotion. John Gill notes the margin of “entreat me not”: “do not meet me,” “be not against me” — do not stand as a hindrance in my way — and Henry turns that margin into doctrine: “we are to reckon those against us, and really our enemies, that would hinder us in our way to the heavenly Canaan,” even when they are our dearest relations. Clarke transcribes the Targum's expansion, in which the verse becomes a proselyte's catechism: Naomi names the sabbath journey, the ban on lodging with Gentiles, the precepts, the prohibition of strange worship — and Ruth answers each with a clause of the verse, whither thou goest I will go, thy God shall be my God. Clarke thinks it “very likely that some such conversation as this took place” between the elders and those becoming proselytes: the synagogue itself heard this verse as the model of conversion.

Henry systematizes what Ruth improvises. She will travel with Naomi, dwell with her, “twist interest with her” — thy people shall be my people — and join in religion with her: “thy God shall be my God, and farewell to all the gods of Moab… this is to take the Lord for our God.” He makes her the “pattern of a resolute convert”: when we take God for our God we must take his people for our people in all conditions, willing “to take our lot with them and to fare as they fare.” And he notices the logic of her faith: judging the nation by the one Israelite she knew, Ruth “concludes certainly that the great nation was a wise and an understanding people” — Henry's quiet charge to every believer to be, like Naomi, a credit to the better country. The wonder of it, all agree with Clarke, is that the attachment was “evidently without any secular motive”: Ruth chooses a destitute widow, a strange land, and the living God, and the choice is one indivisible act.
17:
Ruth seals her confession with death and an oath. Keil and Delitzsch identify the formula — “Jehovah do so to me, and more also” — as the recurring oath of the historical books (1 Samuel 3:17; 1 Samuel 14:44; 1 Samuel 20:13), “by which the person swearing called down upon himself a severe punishment in case he should not keep his word or carry out his resolution”; and they compress the final clause into its true sense: “death alone shall divide between me and thee.” Charles Ellicott draws the theological inference: taking the words literally, “she swears by the name of the God of Israel” — the Moabitess's first recorded act of worship toward Jehovah is an oath in his name — and “with this Naomi yields; after so solemn a protest she can urge no more.”

The burial clause moves the witnesses most. John Gill: she was determined to abide with Naomi unto death, desiring to die in the same country, cottage, and bed, “and be laid in the same grave, in hope of rising together at the resurrection of the just,” renouncing all regard to the sepulchres of her fathers — an honour “people in all ages and countries have been fond of” — and he adduces the pagan parallel of intimate friends buried together among Greeks and Romans, as Crates and Polemon were. John Wesley puts it most tenderly: not desiring so much as her dead body carried back to Moab, “Naomi and she having joined souls, she desires they may mingle dust, in hopes of rising together, and remaining together for ever.”

Adam Clarke verifies the vow by the story's end: “it appears that she was true to her engagement; for Naomi was nourished in the house of Boaz in her old age, and became the fosterer and nurse of their son Obed” (Ruth 4:15–16). Matthew Henry presses the one limit the oath concedes: “if aught but death part thee and me” implies that death would part them for a time — “death parts those whom nothing else will part. A dying hour is a parting hour, and should be so thought of by us and prepared for.” Then he turns the figure on the church: “our adherence to Christ must be closer than that of Ruth to Naomi. She resolved that nothing but death should separate them; but we must resolve that death itself shall not separate us from our duty to Christ, and then we may be sure that death itself shall not separate us from our happiness in Christ.” The oath that ended the debate on the Moab road becomes, in the witnesses' hands, the measure of every profession: “fast bind, fast find. He that means honestly does not startle at assurances.”
18:
The long debate ends not with an answer but with a silence. John Gill reads the narrator's clause closely: Naomi “saw that she was steadfastly minded” — strong in her resolutions, steadfast in her determinations, so that “nothing could move her from the firm purpose of her mind” — and he adds the point that reframes the whole dialogue: this steadfastness “was what Naomi wanted to make trial of.” The dissuasions were never opposition; they were assay. Keil and Delitzsch ground the same reading in the verb itself: the word rendered “steadfastly minded” means “to stiffen one's self firmly upon a thing” — Ruth had set herself like something braced against pressure — and so “Naomi gave up persuading her any more to return.”

John Wesley supplies the verse's famous application: “See the power of resolution! Those who are half-resolved, are like a door a-jar, which invites a thief. But resolution shuts and bolts the door, and then the devil flees from us.” Matthew Henry, whose exposition Wesley here distils, says both halves plainly: those who go in religious ways without a steadfast mind “tempt the tempter, and stand like a door half open, which invites a thief; but resolution shuts and bolts the door, resists the devil, and forces him to flee” — the apostle's promise of James 4:7 standing behind the figure. And Henry corrects the surface reading of “she left speaking unto her”: Naomi was not defeated but satisfied. Steadfastness “was the very thing she aimed at in all that she had said,” and when she saw that she had gained her point, “she was well satisfied… She could desire no more than that solemn protestation which Ruth had just now made.”

So the witnesses turn a transitional verse into a small doctrine of perseverance. Temptation, on their common reading, has no purchase on a made-up mind: it was the half-open door of Orpah's warm but unsettled affection through which she went back to Moab, and it is the bolted door of Ruth's oath that, in Henry's phrase, “puts temptation to silence.” The quiet with which the verse closes — no more entreaties, no more recorded tears, only two women walking on together — is the quiet that follows settled conviction; and Naomi's silence is itself a kind of benediction, the elder believer's recognition that the work is done and the convert is the Lord's. From here the narrative can hasten: nothing remains to be decided between them until they stand in Bethlehem.
19:
The narrator compresses a hard journey into half a sentence — “so they two went until they came to Bethlehem” — and the commentators restore the toil. Charles Ellicott: the journey “for two women apparently alone was long and toilsome, and not free from danger”; two rivers, the Arnon and the Jordan, had to be forded, and the distance “cannot have been less than fifty miles.” John Gill notices both their poverty and their fellowship: no camels nor asses to ride, no servants to assist, “such were their mean circumstances”; and though Ruth was the younger and could have gone faster, “she kept company with her ancient mother,” edified, he supposes, by her pious conversation on the road. Matthew Henry adds the timing of Providence: they came “very seasonably, in the beginning of the barley-harvest” — “our times are in God's hand, both the events and the time of them.”

At the gate, commotion. Keil and Delitzsch: “the whole town was in commotion on their account,” and the feminine verbs identify the speakers — chiefly “the female portion of the inhabitants” cried, Is this Naomi? — amazement “not so much at the fact that Naomi was still alive, and had come back again, as at her returning in so mournful a condition, as a solitary widow, without either husband or sons.” Adam Clarke reads the stir socially: Naomi was “not only well known, but highly respected also at Bethlehem; a proof that Elimelech was of high consideration in that place.” Ellicott adds that though news of Bethlehem's plenty had reached Naomi in Moab, no news of Naomi had reached Bethlehem; grief and toil “had doubtless made her look aged and worn.”

Henry hears in the women's question the whole pathos of changed estate. Is this she, John Wesley paraphrases, “that formerly lived in so much plenty and honour? How marvelously is her condition changed?” Henry allows the question two tones. If any asked it in contempt — see what her rambling to a strange country has got her — “their temper was very base and sordid,” for nothing is “more barbarous than to triumph over those that are fallen.” But the generality, he supposes, asked in compassion: is this she that lived so plentifully, kept so good a house, and was so charitable to the poor? “How has the gold become dim!” — like those who had seen the magnificence of the first temple weeping at the meanness of the second. “So unlike is the rose when it is withered to what it was when it was blooming.” And he draws the memento for every reader: “afflictions will make great and surprising changes in a little time… God, by his grace, fit us for all such changes, especially the great change!” The verse sets the stage exactly: a town astir, a broken widow — and beside her, unnamed in the crowd's question, the Moabitess on whom the story, and the line of David, will turn.
20:
Naomi answers the town with a renaming. Charles Ellicott places it among “the constant plays on words and names found in the Hebrew Bible”: Naomi, pleasant — strictly, perhaps, “my pleasantness” — against Mara, bitter, the word of the wilderness spring (Exodus 15:23); and he adds the philologist's caution that Mara “has no connection with Miriam or Mary, which is from a different root.” John Gill corrects Josephus's loose rendering of the pair as “prosperity” and “grief”: the words signify the one “sweet” and pleasant, the other “bitter” — though he grants Josephus rightly observes that she might now more justly be called the one than the other. John Wesley compresses the contrast: Naomi, “pleasant, and chearful”; Mara, “bitter or sorrowful.”

The reason clause carries the theology: “for the Almighty hath dealt very bitterly with me.” Adam Clarke reads the divine name: Shaddai, “He who is self-sufficient, has taken away the props and supports of my life.” Ellicott turns the same derivation into doctrine: the All-Sufficient, “the God who gives all things in abundance is He who takes back” (compare Genesis 17:1). Matthew Henry makes the covenant connection explicit and comforting: Shaddai is “that name of God by which he enters into covenant with his people: I am God Almighty, God All-sufficient” — “he afflicts as a God in covenant,” and “he that empties us of the creature knows how to fill us with himself.” It is folly to contend with the Almighty, Henry adds, and our duty and interest to submit to him.

What the witnesses will not find in the verse is murmuring. Henry's whole reading of vv.20–21 is of “the composure of Naomi's spirit”: she bears the melancholy effects of her affliction “with a great deal of pious patience,” and her renaming is not bitterness against God but agreement with him — “thus does she bring her mind to her condition, which we all ought to do when our condition is not in every thing to our mind.” Many who are impoverished still affect the empty titles of their former honour; “Naomi did not so. Her humility regards not a glorious name in a dejected state.” If God deal bitterly with her, she is willing to be called bitter. “It well becomes us to have our hearts humbled under humbling providences… it is not an affliction itself, but an affliction rightly borne, that does us good” — and he seals it with Seneca's line to Helvia: so many calamities have been lost upon you if you have not yet learned how to suffer. The renamed widow, all the voices agree, is not despairing but truthful — and the book will quietly reverse her word when the women of the same town bless the LORD over Obed, the nourisher and restorer of her life (Ruth 4:14–15).
21:
“I went out full, and the LORD hath brought me home again empty.” Full of what? John Gill collects the readings: full “of my husband and children, as the Targum; of children and riches, as Aben Ezra and Jarchi.” Some Jewish writers, he reports, went further and blamed the family's going abroad on “a covetous disposition, and an unwillingness to relieve the poor that came to them in their distress,” holding their deaths a punishment for it — “but this is said,” Gill judges, “without any just cause or reason that appears.” Adam Clarke takes the fulness economically as well as domestically: Elimelech, fleeing famine, “would naturally take his property with him,” and it is likely Naomi subsisted on it in Moab till all was spent — which explains why she returns not merely bereaved but destitute, and why Ruth must presently glean (Ruth 2:2).

The forensic language of the second half arrests the witnesses. “The LORD hath testified against me” — John Wesley: he “hath borne witness, as it were, in judgment, and given sentence against me.” Matthew Henry expands the courtroom figure: when God corrects us he testifies against us and contends with us (Job 10:17), “intimating that he is displeased with us. Every rod has a voice, the voice of a witness.” Naomi names no secondary causes — Moabite air, ten years' misfortune — but carries her case straight to the bench: it is the LORD who emptied her, the Almighty who afflicted her. Henry finds in that very directness her comfort: “nothing conduces more to satisfy a gracious soul under an affliction than the consideration of the hand of God in it” — It is the LORD (1 Samuel 3:18; Job 1:21).

And on the emptiness itself Henry preaches the book's economics of grace. Much of the fulness of our comfort in this world arises from agreeable relations, and she has lost them all: “so uncertain is all that which we call fulness in the creature” — they that were full have hired out themselves for bread (1 Samuel 2:5). “But there is a fulness, a spiritual and divine fulness, which we can never be emptied of, a good part which shall not be taken from those that have it.” The witnesses leave the verse poised on the irony only the reader can see: the woman who says she has been brought home “empty” has come home with Ruth — and in Ruth, with the ancestress of David and of David's greater Son standing silently at her side. The LORD who testified against her is already, quietly, preparing to testify for her.
22:
The chapter closes with the narrator's summary and a date. John Gill defends the summary against over-reading: Aben Ezra took “so Naomi returned” for a second return at another time, “but it is only an observation of the writer of this history, to excite the attention of the reader to this remarkable event, and particularly to what follows.” Adam Clarke fixes the date: the barley harvest “began immediately after the passover,” held on the fifteenth of Nisan, “which corresponds nearly with our March” — the beginning of spring; Charles Ellicott puts it about the end of April, noting that barley was the earliest of the harvests (Exodus 9:31–32). Both prize the Targum's liturgical gloss: “they came to Beth-lehem on that day in which the children of Israel began to mow the sheaf of barley which was to be waved before the Lord” — the two widows enter the town as the first-fruits are being cut.

The witnesses agree that the date is design, not decoration. Clarke: “this circumstance is the more distinctly marked, because of Ruth's gleaning, mentioned in the succeeding chapter”; Ellicott: “God had restored plenty to His people, and the wayfarers thus arrive to witness and receive their share of the blessing”; and Matthew Henry, expounding the arrival, calls it seasonable twice over — Naomi's own eyes could now confirm what she had heard in Moab, that the LORD had visited his people in giving them bread, and “now they had opportunity to provide for winter. Our times are in God's hand, both the events and the time of them.” Nothing in the story is accident: the famine's end, the season of arrival, and the field of Boaz lie along one providential line.

Clarke ends the chapter with an argument and an extract. The argument is literary and apologetic: “the native, the amiable simplicity, in which the story of the preceding chapter is told, is a proof of its genuineness. There are several sympathetic circumstances recorded here which no forger could have invented. There is too much of nature to admit any thing of art.” The extract, from Mr. Ness's History and Mystery of the Book of Ruth, takes up Naomi's word “rest” (v.9; Ruth 3:1): marriage is “the port or haven of young people,” of all civil affairs the one of greatest importance, wherein an error “is irrecoverable” — “good advice,” Clarke remarks dryly, “but who among the persons concerned will have grace enough to take it?” So a chapter that began with famine and three graves ends with a full harvest and a standing designation: “Ruth the Moabitess… which returned out of the country of Moab.” The narrator keeps her foreign name in view precisely because grace is about to make it glorious.
